children.name: Araneus diadematus
children.description: Classic garden spider of Northern Europe; white cross on abdomen. Common throughout Sweden July-October. Builds new orb web each morning and consumes the old one.

children.name: Araneus cavaticus
children.description: The species immortalised as Charlotte in E.B. White's Charlotte's Web (1952). Nocturnal; builds webs in barns in eastern North America.

children.name: Araneus quadratus
children.description: One of the heaviest orb-weavers in Europe, with four conspicuous white spots on a colour-variable abdomen. Females swell enormously before egg-laying in late summer in grassland and heath.

children.name: Araneus marmoreus
children.description: A vividly marbled orb-weaver, often bright orange in the 'pyramidatus' form. Found in damp woodland edges across the Holarctic; spins its web among low vegetation and hides in a silk retreat.

children.name: Araneus angulatus
children.description: A large orb-weaver with two prominent humps (tubercles) on the shoulders of the abdomen. One of Europe's largest orb-weavers, favouring woodland.

children.name: Araneus gemmoides
children.description: A large North American orb-weaver named for the two horn-like projections on the abdomen that resemble cat ears. Females reach 20mm and build large orb webs in gardens across the western US and Canada.

children.name: Araneus bicentenarius
children.description: One of the largest Araneus in North America, with a greenish abdomen mottled like lichen. Builds huge orb webs exceeding 60cm in diameter in wooded areas of the eastern US.

children.name: Araneus nordmanni
children.description: A large, handsome European orb-weaver with a red-brown abdomen bearing white leaf-like spots. Found in old-growth forests across northern and central Europe.

children.name: Araneus trifolium
children.description: A large, variable-coloured orb-weaver (white, green, yellow, pink) with three white spots forming a shamrock-like pattern on the abdomen. Common across North America in late summer.

children.name: Araneus guttulatus
children.description: A small, pale European orb-weaver with distinctive white spots on a light brown abdomen. Found in damp meadows and marshy areas across central and eastern Europe.

children.name: Argiope bruennichi
children.description: Boldly black-and-yellow striped; now breeding in southern Sweden (Skane). Builds orb web with distinctive zigzag stabilimentum ribbon.

children.name: Argiope aurantia
children.description: Large orb-weaver of North America; vivid yellow-and-black females reach 25mm. Builds webs with vertical stabilimentum.

children.name: Argiope trifasciata
children.description: A widespread orb-weaver with silvery, finely banded abdomen, building large webs in open grassy areas late in the season. Nearly cosmopolitan in warm regions.

children.name: Argiope lobata
children.description: A striking Mediterranean and African orb-weaver with a silvery, deeply scalloped abdomen bearing lobed projections. Builds large webs with a zigzag silk stabilimentum.

children.name: Argiope argentata
children.description: A silver-bodied orb-weaver of the Americas that decorates its web with a distinctive X-shaped silk cross. Common in arid and coastal scrub from the southern US to Argentina.

children.name: Argiope keyserlingi
children.description: An Australian orb-weaver named for the bold white silk cross it builds in its web, sitting at the centre with legs paired along the arms of the cross.

children.name: Argiope versicolor
children.description: A strikingly coloured Asian Argiope with iridescent blue-green and yellow banding on the carapace and legs. Found in Southeast Asia. Builds a stabilimentum cross in its web.

children.name: Argiope catenulata
children.description: An Asian and Australian orb-weaver with a leaf-like silver abdomen and dark chain-like markings. Widespread in rice paddies and grasslands, where it helps control insect pests.

children.name: Argiope pulchella
children.description: A vividly coloured Asian Argiope with bold yellow, black and white bands across a wide abdomen. Common in gardens across India and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Argiope aemula
children.description: A large Argiope of India and Southeast Asia with a tawny brown and white banded abdomen. Builds the characteristic zigzag stabilimentum across the centre of a large orb web.

children.name: Gasteracantha cancriformis
children.description: Hard spiny abdomen in red, white, or yellow variants. Widespread in the Americas from Florida to Argentina.

children.name: Gasteracantha kuhli
children.description: A small Asian spiny orb-weaver with a hard, shield-like abdomen bearing six pointed spines. Common in gardens and forest edges across Southeast Asia.

children.name: Gasteracantha hasselti
children.description: A brightly coloured spiny orb-weaver of Southeast Asia with a hard armoured abdomen bearing six sharp red or black spines.

children.name: Gasteracantha geminata
children.description: A South Asian spiny orb-weaver with six flattened, leaf-like spines and a bright yellow and black patterned back. Common in Indian gardens and forests.

children.name: Gasteracantha sanguinolenta
children.description: An African spiny orb-weaver with a bright red-orange hard abdomen bearing black spots and six short black spines.

children.name: Nephila pilipes
children.description: One of the world's largest orb-weavers; females exceed 20cm leg span. Golden-yellow webs up to 1.5m diameter. Silk strong enough to trap small birds.

children.name: Nephila clavipes
children.description: The only Nephila of the Americas, famed for its enormous golden-coloured silk webs. Females are far larger than males; the silk is exceptionally strong and has been studied for biomaterials.

children.name: Nephila senegalensis
children.description: A sub-Saharan African golden orb-weaver with conspicuously banded legs, spinning large golden-silked webs in savanna and forest edges.

children.name: Nephila komaci
children.description: The largest known orb-weaving spider, with females reaching 3.8 cm body length and 12 cm leg span. Discovered in 2009 in South Africa and Madagascar. The smallest known species of Nephila by web size but largest by body size.

children.name: Nephila sumptuosa
children.description: An East African golden orb-weaver that lives on the islands of Pemba and Zanzibar. Features a distinctive red or orange colouration on the legs.

children.name: Trichonephila clavata
children.description: An East Asian golden orb-weaver, brightly banded in yellow and blue-grey, that has recently become invasive in the southeastern United States. Builds dense multi-layered golden webs.

children.name: Trichonephila edulis
children.description: A large Australasian golden orb-weaver historically eaten by Indigenous Australians and Pacific peoples. Spins huge yellow-silked webs across forest clearings.

children.name: Trichonephila plumipes
children.description: An Australian and Asian golden orb-weaver with feathery tufts of hair on the legs (plumipes = 'feather-footed'). The abdomen has distinctive yellow and black bands.

children.name: Trichonephila inaurata
children.description: A large golden orb-weaver of southern and eastern Africa, with bright red or orange on the legs and a silvery abdomen. Also known as the 'red-legged golden orb-weaver'.

children.name: Larinioides cornutus
children.description: A common waterside orb-weaver with a leaf-shaped folium pattern on the abdomen. Builds its web on vegetation over streams and ponds across the Holarctic.

children.name: Larinioides sclopetarius
children.description: A grey-marked orb-weaver strongly associated with bridges, buildings and artificial light near water, where it exploits insect concentrations. Highly synanthropic.

children.name: Larinioides patagiatus
children.description: A Holarctic furrow orb-weaver with a dark brown abdomen bearing a white folium border. Associated with waterside vegetation, bridges and man-made structures near water.

children.name: Cyclosa conica
children.description: A small orb-weaver that builds a vertical line of debris and prey remains down the centre of its web, among which it sits camouflaged. Found in woodland across the Holarctic.

children.name: Cyclosa turbinata
children.description: A small orb-weaver that builds a vertical line of debris in its web and sits among it, camouflaged. Widespread in the Americas. The abdomen has a distinctive humped profile.

children.name: Cyclosa octotuberculata
children.description: An Asian Cyclosa with eight small humps on the abdomen. Builds a prominent debris chain in the web. Common in gardens and forests across Japan, Korea and China.

children.name: Cyclosa insulana
children.description: A small orb-weaver with a silvery abdomen and brown pattern. Known to decorate the web with prey remains arranged in a vertical line. Ranges from the Mediterranean through Africa to Japan.

children.name: Mangora acalypha
children.description: A tiny pale orb-weaver with three dark marks on a greenish-white abdomen, building very finely meshed horizontal webs in low vegetation across Eurasia.

children.name: Mangora gibberosa
children.description: A small North American orb-weaver with a green or pale abdomen and a distinctive hump. Builds finely meshed horizontal orb webs in low vegetation in wooded areas.

children.name: Mangora maculata
children.description: A small Neotropical orb-weaver with a spotted abdomen. Ranges from the southern US through Central America. Builds horizontal or gently slanted orb webs.

children.name: Zygiella x-notata
children.description: A synanthropic orb-weaver named for the open sector left in its otherwise complete web, through which a signal thread runs to its retreat. Common on window frames and fences.

children.name: Caerostris darwini
children.description: A Madagascan orb-weaver that spins the largest webs of any spider, spanning rivers up to 25 m wide, using the toughest known biological silk. Discovered in 2009 and named for Charles Darwin.

children.name: Neoscona
children.description: One of the most species-rich orb-weaver genera, found nearly worldwide. They are often the most common large orb-weavers in gardens and urban areas, with a characteristic folium pattern on the abdomen. Adults are active from late summer into autumn.

children.name: Neoscona crucifera
children.description: A large, common North American orb-weaver with a distinctive cross-like folium pattern. Females swell dramatically in autumn before laying a single egg sac. Nocturnal; hides in a silk retreat during the day.

children.name: Neoscona domiciliorum
children.description: A North American orb-weaver with reddish femurs and a variable white or yellow folium. Often found in barns and outbuildings.

children.name: Neoscona arabesca
children.description: A small, common orb-weaver named for the arabesque (scroll-like) pattern on its abdomen. Widespread in North America; often the most numerous orb-weaver in grasslands.

children.name: Neoscona adianta
children.description: A small, boldly patterned orb-weaver of open heathland and dry meadows across Europe. Active during the day, unlike most orb-weavers.

children.name: Neoscona nautica
children.description: A nearly cosmopolitan synanthropic orb-weaver. Its specific name 'nautica' refers to its tendency to travel on ships, making it one of the most widespread spider species.

children.name: Neoscona oaxacensis
children.description: A large orb-weaver of the western United States and Mexico, with a dark folium pattern on a light background. Common in gardens and around buildings in the Southwest.

children.name: Neoscona theisi
children.description: A widespread Asian and Australian orb-weaver found from India to the Pacific Islands. Highly variable in colour but always with a distinct folium on the abdomen.

children.name: Neoscona subfusca
children.description: A medium-sized orb-weaver of Africa and Asia with a subdued grey-brown folium pattern. Common in arid and semi-arid regions.

children.name: Neoscona moreli
children.description: A Neotropical orb-weaver ranging from Florida to Brazil. The abdomen has a distinctive white or yellow folium bordered with dark brown.

children.name: Neoscona sicyoides
children.description: An Old World orb-weaver with a broad, flattened abdomen and a prominent dark folium. Active at night in gardens and forests across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Micrathena
children.description: A genus of small to medium orb-weavers with heavily armoured, spine-studded abdomens. Most species are Neotropical, with a few reaching North America. They build vertical orb webs in forest understorey and typically sit in the centre of the web.

children.name: Micrathena gracilis
children.description: A small white-and-black orb-weaver with five pairs of pointed spines on the abdomen. The female has a dramatically armoured, almost shield-like appearance. Common in wooded areas across eastern North America.

children.name: Micrathena mitrata
children.description: A chalky white Micrathena with fewer but longer spines than M. gracilis. Ranges from Mexico to Brazil. Builds its web in light gaps in tropical forests.

children.name: Micrathena sagittata
children.description: A brilliantly coloured red-and-yellow Micrathena with arrow-shaped lateral spines. One of the most striking North American orb-weavers, found in open woodland.

children.name: Micrathena schreibersi
children.description: A large Neotropical Micrathena with a black abdomen bearing bright yellow or white bands and long curved spines. Ranges from Panama to Brazil.

children.name: Micrathena cyrus
children.description: A central and southern South American Micrathena with a black abdomen studded with short, blunt spines and three pairs of white spots.

children.name: Eriophora
children.description: A genus of large, colourful orb-weavers primarily found in the Neotropics, though one species occurs in Australia. They build large, strong orb webs at night and eat the web each dawn. Their leg span can exceed 10 cm.

children.name: Eriophora ravilla
children.description: A large, hairy orb-weaver ranging from the southern US through Central America. Nocturnal; the female can exceed 25 mm body length. The abdomen is highly variable in colour from grey to bright orange.

children.name: Eriophora fuliginea
children.description: A large, dark Neotropical orb-weaver with a velvety grey-brown abdomen and distinct folium. Builds very large webs in tropical forests from Venezuela to Argentina.

children.name: Eriophora transmarina
children.description: The most common large orb-weaver in Australian gardens, also found in New Guinea and the Pacific Islands. Nocturnal; takes down its web at dawn and rests under leaves. Highly variable in colour and pattern.

children.name: Acanthepeira
children.description: A small genus of distinctive orb-weavers with a hardened, star-shaped or toothed abdomen. The carapace and legs are often covered in bristly hairs. They build classic orb webs in open, sunny areas.

children.name: Acanthepeira stellata
children.description: A striking North American orb-weaver whose abdomen extends into five pointed tubercles, giving it a star-shaped profile. The body is grey-brown with a white dorsal pattern. Found in dry, open fields.

children.name: Acanthepeira venusta
children.description: Similar to A. stellata but smaller, with a more rounded star shape. Found in the southeastern United States in meadow and forest edge habitats.

children.name: Verrucosa
children.description: A predominantly Neotropical genus of colourful orb-weavers with a distinctively triangular abdomen that is wider at the rear. They build typical orb webs but often sit with their head downward at the web hub.

children.name: Verrucosa arenata
children.description: A strikingly beautiful North American orb-weaver with a triangular abdomen coloured bright white, pink or yellow with a dark V-shaped mark. Builds vertical orb webs in woodland clearings. The only Verrucosa in the US and Canada.

children.name: Verrucosa undecimvariolata
children.description: A South American orb-weaver with a triangular abdomen bearing eleven pairs of wart-like tubercles. Colour varies from green to brown with white markings.

children.name: Metepeira
children.description: A genus of small to medium orb-weavers known for building complex, three-dimensional webs with the orb suspended within a maze of barrier threads. Some species are colonial, with many individuals building interconnected webs.

children.name: Metepeira labyrinthaea
children.description: A distinctive North American orb-weaver that builds a dome-shaped orb web enclosed in a maze of silk threads. The spider sits in a retreat above the main web. Widespread in the southwestern US and Mexico.

children.name: Metepeira spinipes
children.description: A South American orb-weaver known for forming communal colonies where dozens of individuals share a massive tangle web, each maintaining its own orb within the structure.

children.name: Metepeira grandis
children.description: A large Metepeira ranging from Mexico to Costa Rica. The elaborate web complex includes both a horizontal orb and a labyrinth of barrier silk above and below.

children.name: Kaira
children.description: A genus of kleptoparasitic orb-weavers that build minimal own webs but instead steal silk and prey from the webs of other orb-weavers, particularly those of Leucauge and Tetragnatha. Their white or silvery bodies provide camouflage on host webs.

children.name: Kaira alba
children.description: A small, pure-white orb-weaver that lives as a kleptoparasite on the webs of larger spiders. Invades host webs at night to steal small prey items and even silk from the host's web.

children.name: Kaira altiventer
children.description: A Brazilian kleptoparasitic orb-weaver with a raised, dome-shaped white abdomen. Often found in the webs of Nephila and other large orb-weavers.

children.name: Eustala
children.description: A genus of camouflaged orb-weavers that closely resemble broken twigs or plant stems when at rest. The abdomen is elongated and often bears humps. By day they hide on vegetation with legs pulled tight against the body.

children.name: Eustala anastera
children.description: A well-camouflaged North American orb-weaver whose elongated, grey-brown abdomen bears two humps. Sits motionless on tree bark by day, legs pressed flat, resembling a twig. Builds web at night.

children.name: Eustala cepina
children.description: A South American Eustala with lichen-green and brown mottling for camouflage on tree trunks. Rests in a head-down, stick-like posture during daylight.

children.name: Eustala rosae
children.description: A Neotropical Eustala with a rosy or reddish-brown abdomen bearing small humps. Ranges from Mexico to Argentina. Nocturnal; its cryptically coloured body is invisible on bark.

children.name: Wagneriana
children.description: A Neotropical genus of colourful, patterned orb-weavers whose rounded, often brightly marked abdomen bears small humps or tubercles. They build strong orb webs in forest understorey.

children.name: Wagneriana tauricornis
children.description: A Brazilian orb-weaver with a rounded abdomen bearing paired horn-like tubercles, giving it a bull-like appearance. Striking yellow, white and brown pattern.

children.name: Wagneriana eupalaestris
children.description: A boldly patterned Neotropical orb-weaver from Brazil with a pale cream abdomen marked with dark brown and red spots.

children.name: Alpaida
children.description: A large genus of orb-weavers primarily found in South America, especially the Amazon basin. They build vertical orb webs in forest clearings and edges. Many species have striking black-and-white patterns.

children.name: Alpaida truncata
children.description: A Brazilian orb-weaver with a truncated (blunt-ended) white abdomen marked with black spots. Builds its web in low vegetation in the Atlantic Forest.

children.name: Alpaida veniliae
children.description: A small orb-weaver of the Amazon basin, the Guianas and Brazil, with a white abdomen bearing a dark grey folium.

children.name: Alpaida leucogramma
children.description: A Colombian and Venezuelan orb-weaver with distinct white longitudinal lines on a dark grey or brown abdomen.

children.name: Parawixia
children.description: A genus of predominantly Neotropical orb-weavers, notable for species that display varying levels of sociality, from solitary to communal web-building. They build strong, wide-meshed orb webs.

children.name: Parawixia bistriata
children.description: A South American social orb-weaver from Brazil and Bolivia. Individuals build interconnected orb webs that form large communal structures, with all spiders emerging at dusk to rebuild simultaneously.

children.name: Parawixia dehaani
children.description: An Australasian Parawixia with dark chevron markings on the abdomen. Found from India through Indonesia to New Guinea. Solitary, builds a large orb web in forest gaps.

children.name: Cyrtophora
children.description: A tropical genus that builds horizontal, dome-shaped or tent-like webs, quite different from the classic vertical orb web. The web consists of a dense horizontal sheet with a network of supporting threads above it.

children.name: Cyrtophora citricola
children.description: A large, distinctive spider that builds a horizontal, dome-shaped web rather than a classic orb. Widespread from the Mediterranean through Africa and Asia to Australia; recently introduced to the Caribbean and Florida.

children.name: Cyrtophora moluccensis
children.description: An Australian and Southeast Asian tent-web spider with a silver and brown patterned abdomen. The horizontal web looks like a silk tent or dome and is often built among shrubs.

children.name: Cyrtophora exanthematica
children.description: A striking black-and-white spotted tent-web spider of Southeast Asia and Australia. Known in Japan as the 'mushroom spider' for the dome shape of its web.
